subject: maths
title: Complex Numbers and Quadratic Equations
book: NCERT Class 11 maths — Complex Numbers and Quadratic Equations

nodes.title: Use the imaginary unit i with i^2 = -1
nodes.brief: Introduce i as a symbol for the square root of -1 satisfying i^2 = -1, the device that lets the equation x^2 + 1 = 0 have a solution.
nodes.quote: Let us denote  $\sqrt{-1}$  by the symbol *i*.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — i is defined by i^2 = -1.
No real number squares to a negative, so x^2 + 1 = 0 has no real solution. Introduce a new symbol i = sqrt(-1), so that i^2 = -1. With this, i is a solution of x^2 + 1 = 0. This single rule is the foundation of all complex-number arithmetic.

PART 2 — Always replace i^2 by -1.
Whenever i^2 appears in a calculation, substitute -1. For example i^2 + 3 = -1 + 3 = 2, and 5i^2 = -5. The most common slip is leaving i^2 unsimplified or treating i as a real number whose square is positive.
keyMoves: Write i = sqrt(-1) and hence i^2 = -1
Recognise i as a solution of x^2 + 1 = 0
Replace i^2 by -1 whenever it occurs in a calculation
misconceptions: Treating i as just another real number whose square is positive
Forgetting to substitute i^2 = -1 and leaving i^2 in an answer

nodes.title: Define a complex number as a + ib
nodes.brief: Recognise any number of the form a + ib, with a and b real, as a complex number.
nodes.quote: A number of the form a + ib, where a and b are real numbers, is defined to be a
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Standard form a + ib with a, b real.
A complex number is any number a + ib where a and b are REAL numbers and i is the imaginary unit. a is the term without i; b is the real coefficient of i. Examples: 2 + 3i, -1 + sqrt3 i, 4 - (1/11)i. Both a and b must be real (not themselves complex).

PART 2 — Real and purely imaginary as special cases.
Real numbers are the case b = 0 (e.g. 5 = 5 + 0i). Purely imaginary numbers are the case a = 0 (e.g. 3i = 0 + 3i). Every complex quantity is eventually written in this a + ib form.
keyMoves: Write a complex number in the standard form a + ib with a, b real
Identify a as the term without i and b as the coefficient of i
See real numbers (b = 0) and purely imaginary numbers (a = 0) as special cases
misconceptions: Thinking a or b may themselves be complex rather than real
Confusing the imaginary part with ib instead of the real coefficient b

nodes.title: Identify the real part Re z and imaginary part Im z
nodes.brief: Read off the real part and imaginary part of z = a + ib as the real numbers a and b.
nodes.quote: *a* is called the *real part*, denoted by Re *z* and *b* is called the *imaginary part* denoted by Im *z* of the complex number *z*
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Re z = a, Im z = b, both real.
For z = a + ib, the real part is Re z = a and the imaginary part is Im z = b. BOTH are real numbers. Example: z = 2 + 5i gives Re z = 2 and Im z = 5.

PART 2 — The imaginary part is b, NOT ib.
Im(2 + 5i) = 5, not 5i. The imaginary part is the real coefficient of i, not the term ib. This split into Re z and Im z is the first step in almost every complex-number problem (e.g. equating real and imaginary parts).
keyMoves: Read off Re z = a and Im z = b from z = a + ib
Report Im z as the real coefficient b, not ib
Apply to examples, e.g. Re(2 + i5) = 2, Im(2 + i5) = 5
misconceptions: Taking the imaginary part of a + ib to be ib instead of b
Thinking Re z or Im z can be complex rather than real numbers

nodes.title: Apply the equality condition for two complex numbers
nodes.brief: Use the rule that two complex numbers are equal exactly when their real parts and imaginary parts separately agree, to solve for unknown reals.
nodes.quote: Two complex numbers  $z_1 = a + ib$  and  $z_2 = c + id$  are equal if a = c and b = d.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Two complex numbers are equal iff real parts AND imaginary parts match.
z1 = a + ib equals z2 = c + id precisely when a = c AND b = d. So a single complex equation splits into TWO real equations (one from the real parts, one from the imaginary parts).

PART 2 — Use this to solve for unknown reals.
Given 4x + i(3x - y) = 3 + i(-6): equate real parts 4x = 3 (so x = 3/4) and imaginary parts 3x - y = -6 (so y = 3x + 6 = 33/4). The error to avoid is matching the real part of one side with the imaginary part of the other — always pair like with like.
keyMoves: Set the real parts equal and the imaginary parts equal separately
Turn one complex equation into two real equations
Solve the resulting real equations simultaneously for the unknowns
misconceptions: Equating the whole expressions without separating real and imaginary parts
Matching the real part of one side with the imaginary part of the other

nodes.title: Add and subtract complex numbers component-wise
nodes.brief: Add (and subtract) complex numbers by combining real parts with real parts and imaginary parts with imaginary parts.
nodes.quote: which is again a complex number.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Add component-wise: real with real, imaginary with imaginary.
For z1 = a + ib and z2 = c + id, the sum is (a + c) + i(b + d). Add the real parts and add the imaginary parts separately. Example: (2 + 3i) + (-6 + 5i) = (2-6) + (3+5)i = -4 + 8i.

PART 2 — Subtract via the additive inverse, watching signs.
z1 - z2 = z1 + (-z2), where -z2 = -c - di. Example: (6 + 3i) - (2 - i) = (6-2) + (3-(-1))i = 4 + 4i. The main error is mishandling the sign when negating z2, e.g. forgetting -(-i) = +i.
keyMoves: Compute z1 + z2 = (a + c) + i(b + d) by adding parts separately
Subtract via z1 - z2 = z1 + (-z2) with -z2 = -c + i(-d)
Use that the sum/difference of two complex numbers is again complex
misconceptions: Mixing real and imaginary parts when combining (adding a to d, etc.)
Mishandling signs when negating z2 in a subtraction

nodes.title: Multiply two complex numbers using i^2 = -1
nodes.brief: Multiply complex numbers by expanding the product and replacing i^2 by -1, giving (ac - bd) + i(ad + bc).
nodes.quote: The multiplication of complex numbers possesses the following properties, which we state without proofs.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Multiply like binomials, then replace i^2 by -1.
For z1 = a + ib and z2 = c + id, expand (a + ib)(c + id) = ac + adi + bci + bd i^2. Since i^2 = -1, the bd i^2 term becomes -bd. Collect: z1 z2 = (ac - bd) + (ad + bc)i.

PART 2 — Use the formula and watch the real-part sign.
The real part is ac - bd (note the MINUS, from i^2 = -1), the imaginary part is ad + bc. Example: (3 + 5i)(2 + 6i) = (6 - 30) + (18 + 10)i = -24 + 28i. The main error is forgetting i^2 = -1 and writing the real part as ac + bd.
keyMoves: Expand (a + ib)(c + id) as binomials and replace i^2 by -1
Apply the formula z1 z2 = (ac - bd) + i(ad + bc)
Collect into standard real-plus-imaginary form
misconceptions: Forgetting i^2 = -1 and leaving the real part as ac + (something) bd
Multiplying only the real parts and only the imaginary parts separately

nodes.title: Evaluate powers of i using the cycle of four
nodes.brief: Reduce any integer power of i (positive or negative) using the four-step cycle 1, i, -1, -i.
nodes.quote: where the integer  $k, i^{4k} = 1, i^{4k+1} = i, i^{4k+2} = -1, i^{4k+3} = -i$
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Powers of i cycle every four: i, -1, -i, 1.
i^1 = i, i^2 = -1, i^3 = -i, i^4 = 1, then the pattern repeats. To evaluate i^n, reduce the exponent n modulo 4: remainder 0 gives 1, 1 gives i, 2 gives -1, 3 gives -i. Example: i^22, since 22 = 4*5 + 2, equals i^2 = -1.

PART 2 — Negative powers use the same four-cycle.
i^(-1) = -i, i^(-2) = -1, i^(-3) = i, i^(-4) = 1. Reduce the negative exponent modulo 4 too: i^(-35), since -35 = 4(-9) + 1, equals i^1 = i. The common error is reducing modulo 2 instead of modulo 4.
keyMoves: Use i^1 = i, i^2 = -1, i^3 = -i, i^4 = 1 as the base cycle
Reduce the exponent modulo 4 to pick the value
Handle negative exponents with i^(-1) = -i (same four-cycle)
misconceptions: Thinking the powers of i grow without repeating
Reducing the exponent modulo 2 instead of modulo 4

nodes.title: Find the multiplicative inverse of a non-zero complex number
nodes.brief: Compute 1/z for a non-zero z = a + ib using the formula a/(a^2+b^2) + i(-b)/(a^2+b^2).
nodes.quote: For every non-zero complex number z = a + ib or
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The inverse is conjugate over modulus-squared.
Every non-zero z = a + ib has inverse z^(-1) = conjugate(z)/|z|^2 = (a - ib)/(a^2 + b^2). Equivalently z^(-1) = a/(a^2+b^2) + (-b)/(a^2+b^2) i. It satisfies z * z^(-1) = 1.

PART 2 — Find it by multiplying 1/z by the conjugate.
In practice: 1/(a+ib) = (a-ib)/((a+ib)(a-ib)) = (a-ib)/(a^2+b^2). Example: inverse of 2 - 3i is (2+3i)/(4+9) = (2+3i)/13 = 2/13 + (3/13)i. The errors to avoid: inverting parts separately (1/a + 1/(ib)) and dropping the sign change on the imaginary part.
keyMoves: Apply z^(-1) = a/(a^2+b^2) + i(-b)/(a^2+b^2) for z = a + ib (z not 0)
Equivalently compute conjugate(z)/|z|^2
Check the result by verifying z * z^(-1) = 1 + i0
misconceptions: Writing 1/(a + ib) = 1/a + 1/(ib) as if the parts invert separately
Omitting the sign change on the imaginary part (-b in the numerator)

nodes.title: Divide one complex number by another
nodes.brief: Compute the quotient z1/z2 (z2 not zero) as z1 times the inverse of z2, equivalently by multiplying by the conjugate of the denominator.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Divide by multiplying by the conjugate of the denominator.
For z1/z2 (z2 != 0), multiply numerator and denominator by the conjugate of z2. This makes the denominator the real number |z2|^2, clearing i from the bottom. z1/z2 = z1 * conjugate(z2) / |z2|^2.

PART 2 — Reduce to standard a + ib form.
Example: (6 + 3i)/(2 - i) = (6 + 3i)(2 + i)/((2-i)(2+i)) = (12 + 6i + 6i + 3i^2)/(4+1) = (9 + 12i)/5 = 9/5 + (12/5)i. Never divide real and imaginary parts separately, and never leave i in the denominator.
keyMoves: Write z1/z2 = z1 * (1/z2) for z2 not equal to 0
Multiply numerator and denominator by the conjugate of the denominator
Simplify to the standard form a + ib
misconceptions: Dividing real parts and imaginary parts separately
Forgetting to rationalise by the conjugate, leaving i in the denominator

nodes.title: Express the square root of a negative real number
nodes.brief: Write the square root of a negative real number using i: for a > 0, sqrt(-a) = sqrt(a) i, and identify both square roots.
nodes.quote: Therefore, the square roots of -1 are i, -i. However, by the symbol  $\sqrt{-1}$ , we would mean *i* only.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — sqrt(-a) = sqrt(a) i for a > 0.
Since i^2 = -1, the square root of a negative real number is written using i: for a > 0, sqrt(-a) = sqrt(a) * sqrt(-1) = sqrt(a) i. Example: sqrt(-9) = 3i, sqrt(-2) = sqrt(2) i.

PART 2 — The two roots, and the convention.
The two square roots of -1 are i and -i (both square to -1), but by convention the symbol sqrt(-1) means i only. Always convert sqrt of a negative to i-form FIRST, before any multiplying or adding — this avoids the sqrt(a)sqrt(b) pitfall for two negatives.
keyMoves: Use sqrt(-1) = i (the two square roots of -1 are i and -i)
Write sqrt(-a) = sqrt(a) i for a > 0
Convert roots of negatives to i-form before combining, e.g. sqrt(-2) = sqrt(2) i
misconceptions: Leaving sqrt(-a) as a real number instead of introducing i
Taking sqrt(-1) to mean -i rather than the conventional value i

nodes.title: Avoid the sqrt(a)·sqrt(b) = sqrt(ab) pitfall for negatives
nodes.brief: Recognise that the identity sqrt(a)·sqrt(b) = sqrt(ab) fails when both a and b are negative, and convert to i-form first.
nodes.quote: if both *a* and *b* are negative real numbers.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — sqrt(a)sqrt(b) = sqrt(ab) FAILS when both a and b are negative.
The rule holds for non-negative a, b (and when exactly one is negative), but breaks for two negatives. If it held for all reals, then i^2 = sqrt(-1)sqrt(-1) = sqrt((-1)(-1)) = sqrt(1) = 1, contradicting i^2 = -1. So you cannot combine two negative roots under one radical.

PART 2 — Convert each root to i-form FIRST.
The safe procedure: write each sqrt of a negative with i before multiplying. Example: sqrt(-2)*sqrt(-3) = (sqrt2 i)(sqrt3 i) = sqrt6 i^2 = -sqrt6, NOT sqrt6. Blindly applying the product rule gives +sqrt6, which is wrong.
keyMoves: State that sqrt(a)*sqrt(b) = sqrt(ab) fails when both a and b are negative
Convert each root of a negative to i-form before multiplying
See that applying the rule blindly would force the contradiction i^2 = 1
misconceptions: Applying sqrt(a)*sqrt(b) = sqrt(ab) to two negative numbers
Concluding sqrt(-2)*sqrt(-3) = sqrt(6) instead of -sqrt(6)

refreshers.trigger: Student does not recall the surd product rule sqrt(a)*sqrt(b) = sqrt(ab) for non-negative a and b, which this node corrects for the two-negatives case.
refreshers.surfacingQuestion: quick one before we break it: when both numbers are non-negative, can you multiply two square roots by just multiplying what is inside? like sqrt(4) times sqrt(9)?
ladder: sqrt(4) = 2 and sqrt(9) = 3, so sqrt(4) times sqrt(9) is just 2 times 3 = 6. do you agree so far?
now put both numbers under ONE root: sqrt(4 times 9) = sqrt(36). what does sqrt(36) come out to?
you got 6 both ways. so for these non-negative numbers, sqrt(4)*sqrt(9) and sqrt(36) gave the same answer — the two roots combined into one.
try it once more to be sure the pattern is real: is sqrt(2)*sqrt(8) the same as sqrt(2 times 8) = sqrt(16)? what is each side?
refreshers.answer: For numbers that are zero or positive, you really can merge two square roots into one: sqrt(a)*sqrt(b) = sqrt(ab). That is why sqrt(4)*sqrt(9) = sqrt(36) = 6, and sqrt(2)*sqrt(8) = sqrt(16) = 4 — both routes land on the same value. This is the standard surd product rule you met for radicals, and it is genuinely safe whenever a and b are non-negative (it also still works if just one of them is negative). The whole point of THIS node is that the moment BOTH numbers are negative, this comfortable rule quietly breaks — so we need the rule firmly in hand first, precisely so we can see exactly where and why it stops being allowed.
refreshers.returnCue: good — so the merge-into-one-root rule is solid for non-negatives. now watch what goes wrong the instant BOTH numbers are negative, like sqrt(-2)*sqrt(-3).

nodes.title: Apply algebraic identities to complex numbers
nodes.brief: Use the standard algebraic identities, such as (z1 + z2)^2 and (z1 - z2)^3, which hold for all complex numbers, to expand powers.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Real algebraic identities hold for all complex numbers.
Because complex numbers obey the same distributive and commutative laws as reals, identities like (z1 +/- z2)^2 = z1^2 +/- 2 z1 z2 + z2^2, (z1 +/- z2)^3, and z1^2 - z2^2 = (z1+z2)(z1-z2) all carry over unchanged. You can apply them directly to expand powers of complex numbers.

PART 2 — Reduce powers of i after expanding.
After applying an identity, replace i^2 = -1 and i^3 = -i in each term. Example: (1 + i)^2 = 1 + 2i + i^2 = 1 + 2i - 1 = 2i. Forgetting to reduce i^2/i^3 leaves the answer wrong.
keyMoves: Use that real-number algebraic identities hold for all complex numbers
Apply (z1 +/- z2)^2 and (z1 +/- z2)^3 to expand powers of complex numbers
Substitute i^2 = -1 (and i^3 = -i) while simplifying each term
misconceptions: Doubting the identities hold for complex numbers and expanding term-by-term incorrectly
Forgetting to reduce powers of i (i^2, i^3) after expanding

refreshers.trigger: Student does not recall the standard algebraic identities, especially (a +/- b)^3 = a^3 +/- 3a^2 b + 3a b^2 +/- b^3 and a^2 - b^2 = (a+b)(a-b), needed to expand powers like (z1 +/- z2)^3.
refreshers.surfacingQuestion: quick one before we expand: do you still remember the school identities for (a + b)^2, (a + b)^3, and a^2 - b^2?
ladder: start with the square: (a + b)^2 means (a + b)(a + b). expand the brackets and collect — what three terms do you get?
you should land on a^2 + 2ab + b^2. and for (a - b)^2 the only change is the middle sign: a^2 - 2ab + b^2. can you see why just the cross term flips?
now the cube: (a + b)^3 = (a + b)(a + b)^2. multiply a^2 + 2ab + b^2 by (a + b) and collect — how many terms, and what are they?
last one: multiply out (a + b)(a - b). the two middle terms cancel — what single difference is left?
refreshers.answer: The standard identities are: (a + b)^2 = a^2 + 2ab + b^2 and (a - b)^2 = a^2 - 2ab + b^2 (only the middle term changes sign); (a + b)^3 = a^3 + 3a^2 b + 3ab^2 + b^3 and (a - b)^3 = a^3 - 3a^2 b + 3ab^2 - b^3 (the signs alternate); and a^2 - b^2 = (a + b)(a - b). They come straight from expanding the brackets and collecting like terms, so they hold for any quantities that multiply and add normally. That is exactly why they carry over unchanged to complex numbers: in this node you put z1 and z2 in place of a and b and apply the very same formulas to expand (z1 +/- z2)^2 and (z1 +/- z2)^3 — then you just finish by replacing i^2 with -1 and i^3 with -i.
refreshers.returnCue: good — same identities, now with z1 and z2 in place of a and b. let us expand the power and then tidy up the powers of i.

nodes.title: Compute the modulus |z| of a complex number
nodes.brief: Compute the modulus of z = a + ib as the non-negative real number sqrt(a^2 + b^2).
nodes.quote: the modulus of z, denoted by |z|, is defined to be the non-negative real number
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — |z| = sqrt(a^2 + b^2), always real and non-negative.
For z = a + ib, the modulus is |z| = sqrt(a^2 + b^2). It measures the size of z and is always a non-negative real number; |z| = 0 only when z = 0. Example: |3 + i| = sqrt(9+1) = sqrt(10); |2 - 5i| = sqrt(4+25) = sqrt(29).

PART 2 — Square the imaginary coefficient, and use z*conjugate(z) = |z|^2.
Use b^2, not b: for 2 - 5i the imaginary part contributes (-5)^2 = 25. The key identity z * conjugate(z) = |z|^2 (a real number) is what lets you find inverses and quotients. Do NOT take |z| as a + b.
keyMoves: Apply |z| = sqrt(a^2 + b^2) for z = a + ib
Use that |z| is always real and non-negative
Use the relation z * conjugate(z) = |z|^2
misconceptions: Taking the modulus as a + b instead of sqrt(a^2 + b^2)
Forgetting to square the imaginary coefficient (using b instead of b^2)

nodes.title: Form the conjugate of a complex number
nodes.brief: Form the conjugate of z = a + ib by negating the imaginary part, giving a - ib.
nodes.quote: the conjugate of z, denoted as
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Conjugate: keep the real part, flip the sign of the imaginary part.
The conjugate of z = a + ib is z-bar = a - ib. Only the imaginary part changes sign; the real part is unchanged. Examples: conjugate of 3 + i is 3 - i; of 2 - 5i is 2 + 5i; of -5 - 3i is -5 + 3i.

PART 2 — Why it matters: z * z-bar = |z|^2 is real.
Multiplying z by its conjugate gives the real number |z|^2, which is exactly what clears i from a denominator (used in division and inverses: z^(-1) = z-bar/|z|^2). Do NOT also negate the real part — only the imaginary part flips. The modulus is unchanged: |z-bar| = |z|.
keyMoves: Form z-bar = a - ib by negating the imaginary part of z = a + ib
Use that z * z-bar = |z|^2 is real
Multiply by the conjugate to rationalise a complex denominator
misconceptions: Negating the real part as well as the imaginary part
Thinking the conjugate changes the modulus (|z-bar| = |z|)

nodes.title: Use properties of modulus and conjugate over products and quotients
nodes.brief: Apply the rules |z1 z2| = |z1||z2|, |z1/z2| = |z1|/|z2|, conjugate of a product/quotient/sum distributes, to simplify expressions.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Modulus distributes over products and quotients.
|z1 z2| = |z1| |z2| and |z1/z2| = |z1|/|z2| (z2 != 0). So you can take the modulus of a complicated product/quotient factor by factor, without first reducing to a + ib. BUT modulus does NOT distribute over a sum: |z1 + z2| is generally not |z1| + |z2|.

PART 2 — Conjugation distributes over all four operations.
conjugate(z1 z2) = conjugate(z1) conjugate(z2); conjugate(z1 +/- z2) = conjugate(z1) +/- conjugate(z2); conjugate(z1/z2) = conjugate(z1)/conjugate(z2). So you may conjugate term by term. Remember to conjugate EVERY factor.
keyMoves: Use |z1 z2| = |z1||z2| and |z1/z2| = |z1|/|z2| to split a modulus over factors
Distribute conjugation over products, quotients and sums
Combine these to find the modulus or conjugate of a complicated expression directly
misconceptions: Thinking |z1 + z2| = |z1| + |z2| (modulus does not split over a sum)
Failing to conjugate every factor in a product or quotient

nodes.title: Represent a complex number as a point in the Argand plane
nodes.brief: Plot z = x + iy as the point (x, y) in the complex (Argand) plane, with the modulus as its distance from the origin.
nodes.quote: The plane having a complex number assigned to each of its point is called the *complex plane* or the *Argand plane*.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Plot x + iy as the point (x, y) in the Argand plane.
Each complex number x + iy corresponds to the point P(x, y) in the plane. The horizontal axis (numbers a + 0i) is the REAL axis; the vertical axis (numbers 0 + bi) is the IMAGINARY axis. Do not swap them: the real part is the x-coordinate.

PART 2 — The modulus is the distance from the origin.
|x + iy| = sqrt(x^2 + y^2) is exactly the distance of P(x, y) from the origin O. So |z| measures how far the point is from 0, turning algebraic facts about complex numbers into geometry of points and distances.
keyMoves: Plot x + iy as the point (x, y) in the Argand plane
Identify the real axis (b = 0) and the imaginary axis (a = 0)
Read |z| = sqrt(x^2 + y^2) as the distance of the point from the origin
misconceptions: Swapping the axes and plotting the imaginary part on the x-axis
Thinking the modulus is a coordinate rather than the distance to the origin

nodes.title: Locate the conjugate as the mirror image across the real axis
nodes.brief: See that the conjugate z = x - iy is the mirror image of z = x + iy in the real axis of the Argand plane.
nodes.quote: Geometrically, the point (x, -y) is the mirror image of the point (x, y) on the real axis (Fig 4.3).
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The conjugate is the reflection across the real axis.
z = x + iy is the point (x, y); its conjugate z-bar = x - iy is the point (x, -y). Geometrically, conjugation reflects the point across the horizontal (real) axis — the x-coordinate stays, the y-coordinate flips sign.

PART 2 — Consequences of the reflection.
Because reflection preserves distance to the origin, |z-bar| = |z|. Also z + z-bar = 2x (real, twice the real part) and z - z-bar = 2iy (purely imaginary). Note the reflection is across the REAL axis, not the imaginary axis.
keyMoves: Plot z = x + iy at (x, y) and z-bar = x - iy at (x, -y)
Recognise conjugation as reflection across the real axis
Use that |z| = |z-bar| since the reflection preserves distance to the origin
misconceptions: Reflecting across the imaginary axis instead of the real axis
Thinking the conjugate lies at a different distance from the origin than z

nodes.title: Reduce any complex expression to the standard form a + ib
nodes.brief: Combine the algebra of complex numbers (add, multiply, powers of i, inverse, division, conjugate) to reduce any given expression to the standard form a + ib.
nodes.quote: Equating the real and the imaginary parts of (1), we get
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Reduce any complex expression to the single form a + ib.
The overarching skill: take any complex expression (product, power, quotient, root of a negative, conjugate) and rewrite it as a + ib, then read off Re and Im. Use the full toolkit: add/subtract component-wise, multiply with i^2 = -1, evaluate powers of i by the four-cycle, and divide by multiplying by the conjugate of the denominator.

PART 2 — Rationalise quotients and watch the sqrt pitfall.
Always clear i from any denominator by multiplying by the conjugate, and convert sqrt of a negative to i-form first (sqrt(a)sqrt(b) is NOT sqrt(ab) for two negatives). Once in a + ib form, you can equate real and imaginary parts, or read off |z| and the conjugate. Do not stop before cleanly separating real part a and imaginary part b.
keyMoves: Apply add/subtract, multiply (i^2 = -1) and powers-of-i to expand the expression
Rationalise any quotient by multiplying by the conjugate of the denominator
Collect into a + ib and, if needed, equate real and imaginary parts or read off |z| and the conjugate
misconceptions: Leaving i in a denominator instead of rationalising to a + ib
Stopping before separating the result cleanly into real part a and imaginary part b
